variants.finding_summary: TP53 mutations are universal across cancer types. The loss of a tumor suppressor is most often through large deleterious events, such as frameshift mutations, or premature stop codons. In TP53 however, many of the observed mutations in cancer are found to be single nucleotide missense variants. These variants are broadly distributed throughout the gene, but with the majority localizing in the DNA binding domain. There is no single hotspot in the DNA binding domain, but a majority of mutations oc

associated_conditions: Congenital bilateral absence of vas deferens
variants.finding_summary: This gene encodes a member of the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter superfamily. The encoded protein functions as a chloride channel, making it unique among members of this protein family, and controls ion and water secretion and absorption in epithelial tissues. Channel activation is mediated by cycles of regulatory domain phosphorylation, ATP-binding by the nucleotide-binding domains, and ATP hydrolysis. Mutations in this gene cause cystic fibrosis, the most common lethal genetic disorder

variants.frequency_prognosis: Juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (prevalence: 1-9 / 1 000 000). Metachondromatosis. Noonan syndrome (prevalence: 6-9 / 10 000).
variants.finding_summary: The protein encoded by this gene is a member of the protein tyrosine phosphatase (PTP) family. PTPs are known to be signaling molecules that regulate a variety of cellular processes including cell growth, differentiation, mitotic cycle, and oncogenic transformation. This PTP contains two tandem Src homology-2 domains, which function as phospho-tyrosine binding domains and mediate the interaction of this PTP with its substrates. This PTP is widely expressed in most tissues and plays a regulatory

_board_selection_metadata.criteria_summary: ACMG P/LP + HPO-matched rare VUS (SF genes excluded, soft caps, no fallback)

clinical_board.primary_diagnosis: Multiple pathogenic variants identified, requiring further clinical correlation
clinical_board.primary_diagnosis_evidence: The patient carries three distinct pathogenic variants in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes, associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (AD), Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD (AR), and MUTYH-associated polyposis (AR), respectively. The clinical presentation includes broad HPO terms (Seizure, Global developmental delay, Neoplasm) with low specificity. Zygosity for CFTR and MUTYH is unknown. Prior cases with similar findings often resulted in an undetermined etiology due to multiple pathogenic variants.

differential_diagnoses.evidence: Pathogenic TP53 variant identified. Associated with neoplasm (HPO match). Requires clinical correlation for cancer predisposition.

differential_diagnoses.diagnosis: Cystic Fibrosis or CBAVD (due to CFTR variant)
differential_diagnoses.evidence: Pathogenic CFTR variant identified. Requires determination of zygosity. Associated with potential respiratory/digestive/reproductive issues.
Pathogenic MUTYH variant identified. Requires determination of zygosity. Associated with colorectal adenomas/polyps.

differential_diagnoses.diagnosis: Undetermined genetic etiology for Global Developmental Delay
differential_diagnoses.evidence: Presence of Global developmental delay HPO term, but also multiple pathogenic variants in genes not typically associated with GDD as a primary feature, and broad HPO terms limit specificity.
While TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH variants are identified, the clinical picture might involve other conditions or be a complex presentation not fully explained by these variants alone.

key_findings: Three pathogenic variants identified in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH.
Associated conditions: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (AD), Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD (AR), MUTYH-associated polyposis (AR).
Zygosity for CFTR and MUTYH variants is unknown.
Broad and non-specific HPO terms (Seizure, Global developmental delay, Neoplasm) are present.
Prior cases with similar findings often had undetermined etiology due to multiple pathogenic variants.
recommendations: Determine the zygosity status for the CFTR and MUTYH variants.
Perform a thorough clinical evaluation to assess for signs and symptoms related to Li-Fraumeni syndrome, Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD, and MUTYH-associated polyposis.
Consider genetic counseling for the patient and family regarding the implications of these findings and inheritance patterns.
Evaluate the patient's phenotype carefully to determine which variant(s), if any, are contributing to the clinical presentation.
Given the multiple pathogenic variants, functional studies or segregation analysis might be considered if clinically indicated.

findings.finding: Three pathogenic variants have been identified in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes.
findings.evidence: The analysis identified three variants classified as Pathogenic based on ACMG/AMP criteria (PS1, PP5, PM2_Supporting for TP53 and MUTYH; PS1, PP5, BS1 for CFTR). ClinVar also supports the Pathogenic classification for all three variants.

findings.finding: The identified variants are associated with distinct genetic conditions: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53), Cystic fibrosis (CFTR), and MUTYH-associated polyposis (MUTYH).
findings.evidence: OMIM gene-disease associations confirm these links. TP53 is associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (MIM: 191170, AD inheritance). CFTR is associated with Cystic fibrosis (MIM: 602421, AR inheritance). MUTYH is associated with MUTYH-associated polyposis (MIM: 604933, AR inheritance).

findings.finding: The clinical presentation includes Seizure, Global developmental delay, and Neoplasm, which are broad HPO terms associated with many genes, including the ones identified.
findings.evidence: HPO matches were found for Seizure (HP:0001250), Global developmental delay (HP:0001263), and Neoplasm (HP:0002664). However, these terms are very broad and associated with a large number of genes, limiting their specificity for gene-disease correlation in this context.

findings.finding: The identified variants (TP53 c.7675088C>A, CFTR c.117559590ATCT>A, MUTYH c.165342465G>A) have been previously encountered in this analysis mode (rare-disease) with similar diagnostic uncertainty (multiple pathogenic variants identified, requiring further clinical correlation).
findings.evidence: Prior board knowledge indicates these specific variants have been seen before, often in cases with overlapping phenotypes and multiple pathogenic findings, leading to diagnoses like 'Multiple pathogenic variants identified, requiring further clinical correlation' or 'Undetermined genetic etiology for Global Developmental Delay with multiple incidental pathogenic variants'.

findings.finding: The zygosity of the variants is unknown. For autosomal recessive conditions (CFTR, MUTYH), the presence of a single pathogenic variant indicates carrier status, not necessarily disease diagnosis. Two pathogenic alleles are required for diagnosis.
findings.evidence: The case briefing explicitly states that zygosity is unavailable and cautions against assuming affected status for AR conditions based on a single pathogenic variant.

recommendations: Correlate the identified pathogenic variants with the patient's clinical presentation, considering the inheritance patterns (AD for TP53, AR for CFTR and MUTYH).

Determine the zygosity status for the CFTR and MUTYH variants. If heterozygous, the patient is a carrier. If homozygous or compound heterozygous, it supports a diagnosis of Cystic fibrosis or MUTYH-associated polyposis, respectively.
Consider the possibility of multiple diagnoses or overlapping syndromes given the presence of pathogenic variants in three different genes. The clinical picture might be complex.

Given the broad HPO terms and the presence of multiple pathogenic variants, further detailed clinical information and potentially functional studies might be needed to establish a definitive diagnosis or understand the contribution of each variant.
Review the prior board knowledge for these specific variants to understand the context in which they were previously encountered and the diagnostic challenges faced.
concerns: The HPO terms used (Seizure, Global developmental delay, Neoplasm) are extremely broad and have low specificity, making them less helpful for pinpointing a specific diagnosis in the presence of multiple pathogenic variants.
The zygosity information is missing, which is crucial for interpreting the CFTR and MUTYH variants, especially given their autosomal recessive inheritance patterns.
The presence of multiple pathogenic variants complicates the interpretation and may indicate multiple conditions or a complex phenotype.

findings.finding: The patient carries three pathogenic variants in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes.
findings.evidence: Genomic analysis identified three variants classified as Pathogenic: TP53 chr17:7675088:C>A, CFTR chr7:117559590:ATCT>A, and MUTYH chr2:165342465:G>A. ClinVar confirms the pathogenicity of these variants.

findings.finding: The TP53 variant is associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (LFS), an autosomal dominant (AD) condition characterized by predisposition to various cancers and potentially developmental delay.
findings.evidence: TP53 is associated with LFS (OMIM: 191170). The variant is classified as Pathogenic. HPO matches include Neoplasm (HP:0002664), Seizure (HP:0001250), and Global developmental delay (HP:0001263).

findings.finding: The CFTR variant is associated with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) or Congenital Bilateral Absence of the Vas Deferens (CBAVD), an autosomal recessive (AR) condition.
findings.evidence: CFTR is associated with CF and CBAVD (OMIM: 602421). The variant is classified as Pathogenic. The inheritance pattern is AR.

findings.finding: The MUTYH variant is associated with MUTYH-associated polyposis (MAP) or colorectal adenomas, an autosomal recessive (AR) condition.
findings.evidence: MUTYH is associated with MAP and colorectal adenomas (OMIM: 604933). The variant is classified as Pathogenic. The inheritance pattern is AR. HPO match includes Seizure (HP:0001250).

findings.finding: The patient presents with multiple pathogenic variants in genes associated with distinct conditions (LFS, CF/CBAVD, MAP). This complicates the clinical picture.
findings.evidence: The patient has pathogenic variants in TP53 (AD), CFTR (AR), and MUTYH (AR). The clinical presentation needs careful evaluation to determine which, if any, of these conditions are manifest.

findings.finding: The HPO terms 'Seizure' and 'Global developmental delay' are extremely broad and have low specificity, making them less helpful for pinpointing a specific diagnosis in this context of multiple pathogenic variants.
findings.evidence: The analysis notes warnings about the low specificity of HP:0001250 (Seizure) and HP:0001263 (Global developmental delay), as they are associated with a very large number of genes.

recommendations: Thorough clinical evaluation is crucial to determine the patient's phenotype and assess for signs and symptoms related to Li-Fraumeni syndrome (e.g., early-onset cancers, specific tumor types), Cystic Fibrosis (e.g., respiratory, digestive, reproductive issues), and MUTYH-associated polyposis (e.g., colorectal polyps).

Given the presence of pathogenic variants in AR genes (CFTR, MUTYH), determine the zygosity status of the patient for these genes. If the patient is compound heterozygous for CFTR or MUTYH, they would be affected. If heterozygous, they are a carrier. If homozygous, they are affected. This information is critical for diagnosis.
Consider the possibility of multiple conditions being present simultaneously due to the multiple pathogenic variants identified.
Evaluate the patient for potential mosaicism, although this is less likely given the germline analysis mode.
Consult with specialists in oncology (for TP53), pulmonology/gastroenterology (for CFTR), and gastroenterology/genetics (for MUTYH) for comprehensive management.
concerns: The presence of multiple pathogenic variants complicates the interpretation and diagnosis. It is unclear which variant(s), if any, are responsible for the patient's clinical presentation, or if the presentation is due to a combination of factors or an unrelated condition.
The zygosity status for the CFTR and MUTYH variants is unknown, which is essential for diagnosing AR conditions like CF/CBAVD and MAP.
references: OMIM database for gene-disease associations and inheritance patterns.
ClinVar database for variant classification and clinical significance.

It is unclear which variant(s), if any, are responsible for the patient's clinical presentation, or if the presentation is due to a combination of factors or an unrelated condition.", "The zygosity status for the CFTR and MUTYH variants is unknown, which is essential for diagnosing AR conditions like CF/CBAVD and MAP."], "references": ["OMIM database for gene-disease associations and inheritance patterns.", "ClinVar database for variant classification and clinical significance.", "HPO database for phenotype matching."], "confidence": "moderate"}

findings.finding: The patient carries three pathogenic variants in the TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes.
findings.evidence: Genomic analysis identified three variants classified as Pathogenic based on ACMG/AMP criteria (PS1, PP5, PM2_Supporting for TP53 and MUTYH; PS1, PP5, BS1 for CFTR). ClinVar also supports the pathogenicity of these variants.

findings.finding: The TP53 variant (chr17:7675088:C>A) is associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (MIM: 191170), an autosomal dominant condition predisposing to various cancers and other conditions.
findings.evidence: OMIM database lists Li-Fraumeni syndrome as an association for TP53.

findings.finding: The CFTR variant (chr7:117559590:ATCT>A) is associated with Cystic Fibrosis (MIM: 602421) and Congenital Bilateral Absence of the Vas Deferens (CBAVD).
findings.evidence: OMIM database lists Cystic Fibrosis as an association for CFTR. The variant is classified as Pathogenic.

findings.finding: The MUTYH variant (chr2:165342465:G>A) is associated with MUTYH-associated polyposis (MIM: 604933), an autosomal recessive condition characterized by colorectal adenomas and polyps.
findings.evidence: OMIM database lists MUTYH-associated polyposis as an association for MUTYH. The variant is classified as Pathogenic.

findings.finding: The patient presents with HPO terms of Seizure (HP:0001250), Global developmental delay (HP:0001263), and Neoplasm (HP:0002664).
findings.evidence: HPO phenotype matching identified these terms. Note that Seizure and Global developmental delay are very broad HPO terms with low specificity.

findings.finding: The identified pathogenic variants (TP53, CFTR, MUTYH) are associated with different inheritance patterns (AD for TP53, AR for CFTR and MUTYH).
findings.evidence: OMIM database provides inheritance information for each gene.

findings.finding: The clinical significance of these findings requires correlation with the patient's clinical presentation and family history.
findings.evidence: The presence of multiple pathogenic variants necessitates careful clinical evaluation to determine the specific diagnosis or combination of conditions.

findings.finding: The prior board knowledge indicates that these variants have been seen before in cases with Global Developmental Delay, but the diagnoses were often 'Undetermined genetic etiology' or 'Multiple pathogenic variants identified, requiring further clinical correlation'.
findings.evidence: Review of prior board knowledge.

recommendations: Recommend comprehensive clinical evaluation, including detailed family history, physical examination, and relevant investigations (e.g., cancer screening, pulmonary function tests, sweat chloride test, colonoscopy) based on the identified pathogenic variants.
Consider genetic counseling for the patient and family regarding the implications of these findings, including inheritance patterns, recurrence risks, and potential management strategies.

Given the presence of pathogenic variants in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH, evaluate the patient for features consistent with Li-Fraumeni syndrome, Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD, and MUTYH-associated polyposis, respectively.

Further investigation may be needed to determine the specific clinical impact of these variants, especially if the patient's phenotype does not fully align with the classic presentations of the associated disorders.
concerns: The HPO terms Seizure and Global developmental delay are extremely broad and have low specificity, making them less helpful for pinpointing a specific diagnosis without further clinical context.
The presence of multiple pathogenic variants complicates the interpretation, as the patient may have multiple conditions or an atypical presentation.
references: OMIM database (Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man).

findings.finding: Three pathogenic variants have been identified: TP53 c.7675088C>A (chr17:7675088), CFTR c.117559590ATCT>A (chr7:117559590), and MUTYH c.165342465G>A (chr2:165342465).
findings.evidence: All three variants are classified as Pathogenic based on ACMG/AMP criteria (PS1, PP5, PM2_Supporting for TP53 and MUTYH; PS1, PP5, BS1 for CFTR). ClinVar also lists these variants as Pathogenic.

findings.finding: The identified variants are associated with distinct genetic conditions: TP53 with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (AD), CFTR with Cystic fibrosis (AR), and MUTYH with MUTYH-associated polyposis (AR).
findings.evidence: OMIM gene-disease associations.

findings.finding: The clinical presentation includes HPO terms: Seizure (HP:0001250), Global developmental delay (HP:0001263), and Neoplasm (HP:0002664).
findings.evidence: HPO matching scores for the variants.

findings.finding: The HPO terms Seizure and Global developmental delay are very broad, associated with a large number of genes (1841 and 1913 respectively). This limits the specificity of these matches for gene-disease correlation.
findings.evidence: Warning message associated with HPO terms in the report.

findings.finding: The ClinGen validity for TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH is Definitive.
findings.evidence: Literature Evidence Domain Data.

findings.finding: Prior board knowledge indicates that these variants (TP53, CFTR, MUTYH) have been seen in multiple cases (total_cases=5) associated with Global Developmental Delay, but a definitive genetic etiology has not been established in those cases, often due to the presence of multiple pathogenic variants.
findings.evidence: Prior Board Knowledge.

findings.finding: The frequency of the CFTR variant (chr7:117559590:ATCT>A) is relatively higher in the gnomAD all population (0.01235) compared to East Asian populations (0.00003). The other variants have very low frequencies in the listed databases.
findings.evidence: Variant Frequencies.

recommendations: Correlate the identified pathogenic variants (TP53, CFTR, MUTYH) with the patient's clinical presentation, considering the known associated conditions (Li-Fraumeni syndrome, Cystic fibrosis, MUTYH-associated polyposis).

Given the presence of multiple pathogenic variants, it is crucial to determine which variant(s), if any, are most likely responsible for the patient's phenotype, especially considering the prior cases with similar findings where a definitive etiology was unclear.
Consider functional studies or segregation analysis (if possible) to further evaluate the pathogenicity and potential contribution of each variant to the phenotype.

Be cautious when interpreting the HPO matches, particularly Seizure and Global developmental delay, due to their low specificity. Use them as supportive evidence rather than definitive diagnostic criteria.
Review the patient's family history and consider genetic counseling, especially for autosomal dominant (TP53) and autosomal recessive (CFTR, MUTYH) conditions.
concerns: The presence of multiple pathogenic variants complicates the interpretation and may make it difficult to pinpoint the primary cause of the patient's symptoms based solely on genetic testing.
The HPO terms used are very broad and may not be specific enough to narrow down the diagnosis effectively.

clinical_board_html:
AI Clinical Board
⚠ AI-Generated — This opinion was generated by a local multi-specialist system powered by Google MedGemma. Final clinical judgment must be made by the attending clinician. Variant classification (ACMG/AMP) was performed by a deterministic engine and is not altered by this opinion. MedGemma is not clinical-grade; this output is for research assistance only, not for diagnostic purposes.
Primary Diagnosis
Multiple pathogenic variants identified, requiring further clinical correlation
The patient carries three distinct pathogenic variants in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes, associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (AD), Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD (AR), and MUTYH-associated polyposis (AR), respectively. The clinical presentation includes broad HPO terms (Seizure, Global developmental delay, Neoplasm) with low specificity. Zygosity for CFTR and MUTYH is unknown. Prior cases with similar findings often resulted in an undetermined etiology due to multiple pathogenic variants.
Confidence: MODERATE Consensus: unanimous
Key Findings
  • Three pathogenic variants identified in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH.
  • Associated conditions: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (AD), Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD (AR), MUTYH-associated polyposis (AR).
  • Zygosity for CFTR and MUTYH variants is unknown.
  • Broad and non-specific HPO terms (Seizure, Global developmental delay, Neoplasm) are present.
  • Prior cases with similar findings often had undetermined etiology due to multiple pathogenic variants.
Differential Diagnoses
DiagnosisLikelihood
Li-Fraumeni syndrome (due to TP53 variant)moderate
Cystic Fibrosis or CBAVD (due to CFTR variant)moderate
MUTYH-associated polyposis (due to MUTYH variant)moderate
Undetermined genetic etiology for Global Developmental Delaymoderate
Other genetic conditionslow
Recommendations
  1. Determine the zygosity status for the CFTR and MUTYH variants.
  2. Perform a thorough clinical evaluation to assess for signs and symptoms related to Li-Fraumeni syndrome, Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD, and MUTYH-associated polyposis.
  3. Consider genetic counseling for the patient and family regarding the implications of these findings and inheritance patterns.
  4. Evaluate the patient's phenotype carefully to determine which variant(s), if any, are contributing to the clinical presentation.
  5. Given the multiple pathogenic variants, functional studies or segregation analysis might be considered if clinically indicated.
Follow-up
  • Re-evaluate diagnosis after determining zygosity for CFTR and MUTYH.
  • Monitor for clinical features associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (cancer screening).
  • Monitor for clinical features associated with Cystic Fibrosis/CBAVD (if compound heterozygous).
  • Monitor for clinical features associated with MUTYH-associated polyposis (if compound heterozygous).
Domain Specialist Opinions
▶ Variant Pathologist MODERATE · 5 findings
  • Three pathogenic variants have been identified in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes.
  • The identified variants are associated with distinct genetic conditions: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53), Cystic fibrosis (CFTR), and MUTYH-associated polyposis (MUTYH).
  • The clinical presentation includes Seizure, Global developmental delay, and Neoplasm, which are broad HPO terms associated with many genes, including the ones identified.
▶ Disease Geneticist MODERATE · 6 findings
  • The patient carries three pathogenic variants in TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes.
  • The TP53 variant is associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (LFS), an autosomal dominant (AD) condition characterized by predisposition to various cancers and potentially developmental delay.
  • The CFTR variant is associated with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) or Congenital Bilateral Absence of the Vas Deferens (CBAVD), an autosomal recessive (AR) condition.
Refs: OMIM database for gene-disease associations and inheritance patterns., ClinVar database for variant classification and clinical significance., HPO database for phenotype matching.
▶ PGx Specialist HIGH · 8 findings
  • The patient carries three pathogenic variants in the TP53, CFTR, and MUTYH genes.
  • The TP53 variant (chr17:7675088:C>A) is associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (MIM: 191170), an autosomal dominant condition predisposing to various cancers and other conditions.
  • The CFTR variant (chr7:117559590:ATCT>A) is associated with Cystic Fibrosis (MIM: 602421) and Congenital Bilateral Absence of the Vas Deferens (CBAVD).
Refs: ACMG/AMP guidelines for variant interpretation., OMIM database (Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man)., HPO (Human Phenotype Ontology).
▶ Literature Analyst MODERATE · 7 findings
  • Three pathogenic variants have been identified: TP53 c.7675088C>A (chr17:7675088), CFTR c.117559590ATCT>A (chr7:117559590), and MUTYH c.165342465G>A (chr2:165342465).
  • The identified variants are associated with distinct genetic conditions: TP53 with Li-Fraumeni syndrome (AD), CFTR with Cystic fibrosis (AR), and MUTYH with MUTYH-associated polyposis (AR).
  • The clinical presentation includes HPO terms: Seizure (HP:0001250), Global developmental delay (HP:0001263), and Neoplasm (HP:0002664).
Pre-analytic filtering: 5 variants → 3 presented to Board
Criteria: ACMG P/LP + HPO-matched rare VUS (SF genes excluded, soft caps, no fallback)
